title: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
I Refused Lisinopril for 3 Years. Here's What Happened to My Blood Pressure.

I've been a nurse for 32 years, and high blood pressure is one of the hardest things I watch patients struggle with.

Not the blood pressure itself. The medication.

The persistent dry cough that wakes them up at night. The dizziness that leads to falls. The ankles so swollen they can't wear normal shoes anymore. The exhaustion so heavy they're asleep on the couch by 7 PM.

I've watched patients follow their doctor's orders for years. Decades. Watched their kidney function decline anyway. Watched them end up on dialysis anyway. Watched some of them die anyway—from the very heart failure the medication was supposed to prevent.

The Personal Toll

About three years ago, it happened to me.

My own blood pressure started creeping up. All those years of long shifts, stress, not taking care of myself the way I tell my patients to—it finally caught up with me.

At first it was just occasional high readings. Then it was consistent. 142/91. 145/93. 147/94.

My doctor pulled up my chart. Looked at the trend. And said the words I'd been dreading my entire adult life. "I'm prescribing Lisinopril. 10 milligrams daily."

My throat tightened. Lisinopril.

The same medication I'd watched destroy my mother's quality of life for nine years. Her fingers first—swelling so bad her wedding ring had to be cut off. Then her ankles. She went from heels to flats to house slippers.

Then the cough started. That dry, hacking cough that interrupted every conversation, woke her up at night, made her sound sick even though it was "just the medication." The dizziness got so bad she fell twice. Once in the kitchen. Once getting out of bed.

Her energy disappeared. A woman who used to host Sunday dinners for the whole family couldn't stay awake past 7 PM. And her kidneys. Year after year, her kidney function declined. Her doctor said it was "expected." Said they'd "monitor it." Creatinine climbing. GFR dropping. Stage 3. Then Stage 4. Then dialysis.

Three times a week. Four hours per session. For two years before her heart gave out anyway. My father's words at the funeral still echo: "The pills didn't save her. They just made her suffer longer."

Choosing a Different Path

So when my doctor handed me that prescription, I smiled politely and said I'd think about it. But I knew I wasn't going to take it. I'd watched too many patients go down that road. I'd watched my own mother go down that road. I wasn't about to follow her path.

So I just dealt with it. Cut sodium. Lost fifteen pounds. Walked every morning. Tried to manage my stress. My numbers? Still 144/92 at my next checkup.

The doctor's office called. Then called again. "Ms. Warren, your blood pressure is Stage 2. Dr. Reeves needs confirmation you're taking the medication." I told them I needed more time. But the truth was, I didn't know what else to do.

Then about four months ago, one of my long-term patients came in for her regular appointment. Eleanor. Sweet woman, early sixties. High blood pressure for over a decade. I'd watched her struggle with it for years—tried everything, nothing gave her more than temporary improvement. She was on two medications and still barely controlled.

But this time she seemed different. Lighter somehow. I asked how she was managing, and she said something I didn't expect. "My numbers are actually good now. Like, consistently good. My doctor took me off one of my medications."

I almost dropped her chart. "What happened?"

She told me she'd found this beetroot supplement. But not just any beetroot—she was very specific about that. She'd tried the ones from the grocery store before. Those didn't do anything. This one was different. "I felt it working within the first half hour," she said. "And by the second month, my readings were better than they'd been in years."

The Science Behind the Solution

I'll be honest. I was skeptical. After 32 years as a nurse, I've heard every miracle cure story you can imagine. Supplements that promise the world and deliver nothing. Patients who swear something worked when really their numbers just fluctuated.

But Eleanor wasn't like that. She was sharp. Skeptical herself. And she seemed so genuinely relieved. Like she'd finally found something that actually worked.

That night, I checked my own blood pressure. 146/93. And I kept thinking about what she'd said. So I looked it up.

Turns out, the science on beetroot and blood pressure is real. Dietary nitrates convert to nitric oxide in your body—and nitric oxide is what tells your blood vessels to relax and dilate. Studies from Duke, Stanford, Johns Hopkins showing meaningful blood pressure reductions. Real universities. Real research. Not fringe pseudoscience.

But then I noticed something. People weren't just saying "beetroot supplements." They were getting specific about which ones.

One person said they tried four different brands before finding one that actually worked. Another mentioned that most beetroot supplements are basically worthless—something about the manufacturing process destroying the active compounds.

I started digging deeper. Turns out, most beetroot supplements use high-heat spray drying. It's cheap and fast. But heat destroys up to 90% of the dietary nitrates—the very compounds that lower blood pressure.

And the dosing? The clinical studies used 400 to 500 milligrams of nitrates daily. Most commercial supplements contain less than 50 milligrams. Some as low as 10. But they don't have to tell you that. The label just says "beetroot powder." No nitrate content disclosed.

So you're taking what you think is beetroot for your blood pressure, but you're really getting a tiny fraction of what actually works—and most of that has been destroyed by heat processing anyway. No wonder the supplements I'd seen patients try never seemed to do anything.

My Results with Rosabella

I ordered one of the popular brands. Took it religiously for six weeks. My blood pressure? 143/91. Barely moved.

I tried another brand. The one with the TV commercials. Eight weeks. Didn't miss a single day. Still 142/90.

I was getting frustrated. The research said beetroot worked. But it wasn't working for me.

So I went back to what Eleanor had told me. She'd been specific about the brand. Said most of them didn't work—but this one did. Rosabella.

I looked them up, expecting to be disappointed again. But they were different.

Cold-extracted, not heat-processed. That preserves the nitrates instead of destroying them.

Standardized to 400 milligrams of nitrates per serving—the actual clinical dose.

Third-party tested, with published results. Certificate of Analysis available. You could see exactly what was in it.

No fillers. No additives. Just one ingredient—organic beetroot.

Made in the USA. My next checkup was in six weeks. If my numbers weren't down, I knew what was coming. The prescription. The path my mother walked.

I ordered it. When it arrived, I took the first dose that morning. Didn't expect much. I'd already been burned twice.

But about twenty minutes later, I noticed something. A lift. Like someone had turned up the oxygen in my blood. The mental fog I usually felt by mid-morning wasn't there. I felt... clear.

That evening, the exhaustion that usually hit me around 6 PM didn't come. I walked to the bathroom. Grabbed my blood pressure monitor. Wrapped the cuff. 141/89.

I stared at it. Checked again. 141/89. It was the first reading under 142 I'd seen in over a year.

One week later: 138/86. I started checking morning, afternoon, and evening. The numbers stayed consistent.

Two weeks: 134/84. My husband noticed I wasn't falling asleep on the couch after dinner anymore. Didn't say anything. Just watched me move around the house with energy I hadn't had in months.

Three weeks: 131/82.

Four weeks: 128/79. I checked it three times that morning because I didn't believe it.

The Final Verdict

When I went back to Dr. Reeves for my checkup, the nurse took my blood pressure. Frowned. Took it again. Then a third time. "126 over 78."

Dr. Reeves pulled up my chart. Looked at the history. Looked at the new reading. Looked at me. "What happened?"

I told her. Cold-extracted beetroot. Standardized to the clinical dose. Third-party tested. The difference between what actually works and what just has "beetroot" on the label. She typed notes. Nodding slowly. "Well," she said, closing her laptop. "Whatever you're doing, keep doing it. These numbers are excellent. No Lisinopril necessary at this time."

No Lisinopril necessary.

I walked to my car. Sat there for a moment. Then called my husband. "126 over 78. No medication." I heard him exhale. Then a long silence. "Thank God, Linda. Thank God."

That was four months ago. My blood pressure this morning? 122/76. Normal. Healthy. No medication.

No persistent cough. No dizziness. No swollen ankles. No watching my kidney function decline year after year. No following my mother's path.

Last weekend my daughter asked me to go hiking with her and the grandkids. A real hike—not a flat walking path, but a trail with elevation. Three miles. Two years ago, I would have made an excuse. Would have been too worried about getting winded, about my blood pressure spiking, about not being able to keep up.

But I said yes. And I did it. The whole trail. My five-year-old grandson held my hand the last half mile, chattering about the birds and the bugs and asking if we could do this again next weekend. My eyes burned. Because the answer was yes.

Final Thoughts

I'm sharing this because I hope it can help someone else the way it helped me.

If you're dealing with blood pressure that won't respond to diet and exercise. If your doctor is pushing medication you're terrified to take. If you've watched a parent or spouse suffer through the side effects and you're desperate not to follow that path.

If you've tried other beetroot supplements and they didn't work—now you know why. Most of them are heat-processed garbage with a fraction of the clinical dose.

Rosabella is different. Cold-extracted. 400 milligrams of nitrates. Third-party verified. The dose and the formulation that actually works.

After 32 years as a nurse, I thought I knew every option out there. Turns out, my patient knew something I didn't.

Here's where I got mine:

https://get.tryrosabella.com/beetroot/subtxt/sp

(They're running a buy one get 1 free promo right now and I just ordered six more bottles.)

— Linda Warren, RN

P.S. — A few things I wish I'd known before ordering: Rosabella has a 90-day money-back guarantee. If it doesn't work for you, you get your money back. No questions asked. I wish I'd known that earlier—would have saved me from wasting months on those other brands that didn't do anything.

Most people pay full price, but right now they're running a promotion with significant savings. That's how I was able to stock up.

The only issue is Rosabella is a small company. They've been getting a lot of attention lately—probably because nurses like me keep telling patients about it—and they can't always keep up with demand.

I want to apologize in advance because when you click the link, you might see they're sold out. I've had patients wait weeks for it to come back in stock. But I'd still suggest checking to see if they have any available.

If you're dealing with high blood pressure and you're terrified of going on medication—if you've watched someone you love suffer through those side effects and you refuse to follow that path—I'd grab some before they're gone.

I went from 147/94 to 122/76 in four months. I'm not following my mother's path. If there's even a chance this could work for you, it's worth trying.

👉 Check if Rosabella is in stock here:

https://get.tryrosabella.com/beetroot/subtxt/sp

She told me she'd found this beetroot supplement. But not just any beetroot—she was very specific about that. She'd tried the ones from the grocery store before. Those didn't do anything. This one was different. "I felt it working within the first half hour," she said. "And by the second month, my readings were better than they'd been in years."

The Science Behind the Solution

I'll be honest. I was skeptical. After 32 years as a nurse, I've heard every miracle cure story you can imagine. Supplements that promise the world and deliver nothing. Patients who swear something worked when really their numbers just fluctuated.

But Eleanor wasn't like that. She was sharp. Skeptical herself. And she seemed so genuinely relieved. Like she'd finally found something that actually worked.

That night, I checked my own blood pressure. 146/93. And I kept thinking about what she'd said. So I looked it up.

Turns out, the science on beetroot and blood pressure is real. Dietary nitrates convert to nitric oxide in your body—and nitric oxide is what tells your blood vessels to relax and dilate. Studies from Duke, Stanford, Johns Hopkins showing meaningful blood pressure reductions. Real universities. Real research. Not fringe pseudoscience.

But then I noticed something. People weren't just saying "beetroot supplements." They were getting specific about which ones.

One person said they tried four different brands before finding one that actually worked. Another mentioned that most beetroot supplements are basically worthless—something about the manufacturing process destroying the active compounds.

I started digging deeper. Turns out, most beetroot supplements use high-heat spray drying. It's cheap and fast. But heat destroys up to 90% of the dietary nitrates—the very compounds that lower blood pressure.

And the dosing? The clinical studies used 400 to 500 milligrams of nitrates daily. Most commercial supplements contain less than 50 milligrams. Some as low as 10. But they don't have to tell you that. The label just says "beetroot powder." No nitrate content disclosed.

So you're taking what you think is beetroot for your blood pressure, but you're really getting a tiny fraction of what actually works—and most of that has been destroyed by heat processing anyway. No wonder the supplements I'd seen patients try never seemed to do anything.

My Results with Rosabella

I ordered one of the popular brands. Took it religiously for six weeks. My blood pressure? 143/91. Barely moved.

I tried another brand. The one with the TV commercials. Eight weeks. Didn't miss a single day. Still 142/90.

I was getting frustrated. The research said beetroot worked. But it wasn't working for me.

So I went back to what Eleanor had told me. She'd been specific about the brand. Said most of them didn't work—but this one did. Rosabella.

I looked them up, expecting to be disappointed again. But they were different.

Cold-extracted, not heat-processed. That preserves the nitrates instead of destroying them.

Standardized to 400 milligrams of nitrates per serving—the actual clinical dose.

Third-party tested, with published results. Certificate of Analysis available. You could see exactly what was in it.

No fillers. No additives. Just one ingredient—organic beetroot.

Made in the USA. My next checkup was in six weeks. If my numbers weren't down, I knew what was coming. The prescription. The path my mother walked.

I ordered it. When it arrived, I took the first dose that morning. Didn't expect much. I'd already been burned twice.

But about twenty minutes later, I noticed something. A lift. Like someone had turned up the oxygen in my blood. The mental fog I usually felt by mid-morning wasn't there. I felt... clear.

That evening, the exhaustion that usually hit me around 6 PM didn't come. I walked to the bathroom. Grabbed my blood pressure monitor. Wrapped the cuff. 141/89.

I stared at it. Checked again. 141/89. It was the first reading under 142 I'd seen in over a year.

One week later: 138/86. I started checking morning, afternoon, and evening. The numbers stayed consistent.

Two weeks: 134/84. My husband noticed I wasn't falling asleep on the couch after dinner anymore. Didn't say anything. Just watched me move around the house with energy I hadn't had in months.

Three weeks: 131/82.

Four weeks: 128/79. I checked it three times that morning because I didn't believe it.

The Final Verdict

When I went back to Dr. Reeves for my checkup, the nurse took my blood pressure. Frowned. Took it again. Then a third time. "126 over 78."

Dr. Reeves pulled up my chart. Looked at the history. Looked at the new reading. Looked at me. "What happened?"

I told her. Cold-extracted beetroot. Standardized to the clinical dose. Third-party tested. The difference between what actually works and what just has "beetroot" on the label. She typed notes. Nodding slowly. "Well," she said, closing her laptop. "Whatever you're doing, keep doing it. These numbers are excellent. No Lisinopril necessary at this time."

No Lisinopril necessary.

I walked to my car. Sat there for a moment. Then called my husband. "126 over 78. No medication." I heard him exhale. Then a long silence. "Thank God, Linda. Thank God."

That was four months ago. My blood pressure this morning? 122/76. Normal. Healthy. No medication.

No persistent cough. No dizziness. No swollen ankles. No watching my kidney function decline year after year. No following my mother's path.

Last weekend my daughter asked me to go hiking with her and the grandkids. A real hike—not a flat walking path, but a trail with elevation. Three miles. Two years ago, I would have made an excuse. Would have been too worried about getting winded, about my blood pressure spiking, about not being able to keep up.

But I said yes. And I did it. The whole trail. My five-year-old grandson held my hand the last half mile, chattering about the birds and the bugs and asking if we could do this again next weekend. My eyes burned. Because the answer was yes.

I'm sharing this because I hope it can help someone else the way it helped me.

title: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
My nephew been on me to post this for two months. I told him it wasn't nobody's business. He said it could save somebody's life. So here I am.

I think I had a stroke last June. At my own welcome home cookout. 

Four years away, first day back with the whole family, and I grabbed the grill brush, got two strokes in, and my right arm just stopped working like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground. 

I grabbed the side of the grill to catch myself. 

For one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

My nephew cut the music. The whole yard went quiet.

And that's when I knew how bad it was, because Al Green don't stop at a cookout unless something is really wrong.

But that day didn't come out of nowhere, so let me back up.

About two years before that cookout, while I was still away, I started noticing things weren't right. 

I was heavy in the mornings like I hadn't slept at all, my hands and feet were always cold, and there was this fog sitting in my head every day that I couldn't shake. 

There was also this weight in my chest that wasn't really pain, just weight, and I couldn't explain it to nobody because I didn't know how to name something I'd never felt before.

I asked around. Talked to whoever I could. 

Everybody said the same thing: that's just getting older. 

That wasn’t good enough for me, so I tried what I could. 

CoQ10, garlic, magnesium, cut my salt way down. 

If anything, I felt worse as the months went by.

Then about six months before the cookout I started waking up in the middle of the night, wide awake with this low-grade dread sitting on me for no reason I could name, feeling like my body was trying to tell me something I couldn't figure out. 

And still, nobody around me could tell me why.

Then came June.

My sister had been planning that cookout since the week I got my date. 

My nephew had the speakers up before noon. My mama was in the kitchen making her potato salad the same way she's made it since '87, the one nobody’s allowed to touch.

And I was on the grill. 

That's my spot, always has been.

White tank, tongs in my hand, that smooth Al Green coming through the speakers. Everything exactly the way it was supposed to be on a day I'd been waiting four years for.

I picked up the grill brush to clean the rack before the chicken went on.

First stroke. Second stroke. Third stroke.

My right arm stopped. Like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground and I grabbed the side of that grill to catch myself, and for one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

My nephew came running, took one look at me, and cut the music. 

The whole yard froze. 

And that's when I knew how bad it was.

I waved everybody off, told them I was fine, had my nephew take over the grill and went and sat in a lawn chair for the rest of the afternoon. 

I barely ate, held my arm against my chest the whole time, laughed when I was supposed to laugh, and acted like nothing happened.

You know how it goes.

I went home that night and asked around again. 

Called people I trusted, people who'd been dealing with blood pressure stuff for years. 

Nobody had a real answer, but everybody had a theory.

Too much heat, dehydration, and pushing yourself too hard. 

But nobody could tell me why my arm just stopped like that, and I could tell by the way people were talking that they were scared too, they just didn't want to say it out loud.

So I went home terrified. 

Terrified to go to sleep, terrified my arm was going to do it again but next time there'd be nothing to grab. 

What if it happened when I was driving? What if nobody was around? 

I'd watched my pops go down from something like this. My brother Ray. I knew what road this looked like and I knew where it ended.

I started waking up, wiggling my fingers just to make sure they still worked. 

My nephew kept calling and I kept telling him I was fine, but I wasn't fine and we both knew it.

He drove over two weeks after the cookout. 

When he walked in I could see it in his face, he thought he was going to lose me. 

First cookout back and this is what happened.

We sat at my kitchen table and I tried to act normal, but my hands were cold when I poured the drinks and he noticed.

"Unc," he said quietly. "You're not okay."

I didn't say nothing for a long time. Then I told him everything.

The 3am, fingers in the dark, and terror of knowing something was going wrong in my body and not being able to stop it or name it or find anybody who could tell me what it was.

He didn't say anything for a long time. Then he said: "I'm going to figure this out."

Four days later he called, voice urgent. "Unc. I think I found something."

When he showed up that weekend he was carrying a bottle.

"Before you say anything," he said. "Just let me talk."

He pulled out Rosabella Organic Beetroot and sat back down at that kitchen table and walked me through everything he'd been reading for two weeks straight. 

About something called nitric oxide.

I ain't never heard nothing like that in my life, but I let him keep going because I could see in his eyes he needed me to listen. 

He explained how the body makes it naturally to keep blood vessels relaxed and open on their own, how it declines every year after 40, how by the time most men get to where I am we done lost most of what we had, and how that decline was connected to everything I'd been feeling.

The heaviness, the cold hands, the fog, the way my circulation had been quietly slowing down for years without me having any name for it.

He said the blood pressure pills they always want to put everybody on deal with the reading but they don't do a thing about that. 

And that one of the most concentrated natural sources of what the body needs to actually make nitric oxide is beetroot.

He slid the bottle across the table. I picked it up and read the label slow.

"Beetroot," I said.

"Beetroot," he said.

Now look. I played defensive end. I ain't trying to hear nothing about no beetroot. 

But I could tell this boy had done his homework, and more than that I could see something in his face I hadn't seen since he was a little boy: fear. 

Not fear of the supplement. Fear of losing me.

This boy who used to sit in the front row at every one of my high school football games. 

Who wrote me letters the whole four years without missing one. 

Who planned that cookout with his mama because he wanted me to come home to something that felt right.

He wasn't trying to sell me something. He was scared.

"Unc I can't watch you go out like pops and Ray. Please."

I said aight.

He told me two capsules every morning with breakfast, made me promise, left the bottle and went home. I didn't call him for two weeks but I kept my word.

Called him on a Tuesday morning and the first thing out my mouth was: "Nephew… What is in this stuff."

Because listen, for weeks my hands had been warm when I woke up, and I ain't even notice how cold they'd been every single morning until the morning they weren't. 

That's how long I'd been living like that without knowing any different.

The heaviness in my chest started lifting too, and something was going on in my body that I couldn't explain, I just knew it was different than it had been in years.

Couple weeks later my sister called my nephew without telling me. 

Said she ain't know what was different but I seemed more like myself, and that there was a stillness she'd been watching in me since I came home that she hadn't said nothing about.

And my sleep got right too. 

I had forgot what it felt like to sleep all the way through without lying there with my thoughts running.

About six weeks in, nephew came by on a Sunday and I was in the driveway washing my truck, something I hadn't done myself in over a year because standing that long wore me out. 

He pulled up and sat in his car for a second before he got out, and I could see him through the windshield just watching me work. 

He got out and didn't say a word. I looked up.

"That stuff works, nephew."

Went right back to washing the truck.

Two months later we had another cookout. 

I was at the grill the whole afternoon, white tank, tongs in my hand, didn't sit down once, didn't come nowhere near that potato salad. 

And for me that's when I knew for real.

It's been about four months now. I take it every morning. 

My blood pressure feels the most stable it's been in years, at least I think. 

My hands are warm. 

I get through a full day without counting down the hours. 

I feel like myself again, and I'm old enough to know that ain't nothing small.

I'm not telling nobody what to do with their health. 

What I'm telling you is that my nephew found something that nobody in my community had ever told me about, and it addressed something that nothing else had touched, and I believe if you been feeling the way I was feeling you deserve to know about it too. 

Because that's how we do. 

When something is real, you tell the people you care about.

I got my neighbor Calvin on it, been dealing with blood pressure stuff for eight years and called me two weeks in saying something was different. 

Got my neighbor Ms. Trice on it, she's 68 and said she got more energy than she can remember having in years. 

I been telling everybody I know.

Before you go looking for a beetroot supplement though, I need to tell you something. 

Most of the ones on Amazon and at the regular stores don't work, and my nephew found that out the hard way testing all of them. 

Most brands use high-heat processing to make it fast and cheap, and that destroys the nitrates before they ever reach your body, so you're basically paying for red powder in a capsule with nothing left in it that matters.

Rosabella cold processes theirs so the nitrates are actually intact when you take it. 

Certified organic whole beetroot root, 1300mg per serving, one ingredient, third-party tested.

They got a 90-day money-back guarantee, so if it doesn't do anything for you, you send it back. 

But if it does what it did for me, you'll know.

I'm not somebody who posts things on the internet. Ask anybody who knows me. 

But nephew said somebody out here needs to hear this, and I believe he's right.

He put me on a subscription after the second month. 

Brings it to the house himself. I keep telling him I'm going to pay him back, but he keeps telling me not to worry about it.

👉 get.tryrosabella.com/beetroot/subtxt/mm-hbp-ps

My throat tightened. Lisinopril.

Not the blood pressure itself. The medication.

The persistent dry cough that wakes them up at night. The dizziness that leads to falls. The ankles so swollen they can't wear normal shoes anymore. The exhaustion so heavy they're asleep on the couch by 7 PM.

I've watched patients follow their doctor's orders for years. Decades. Watched their kidney function decline anyway. Watched them end up on dialysis anyway. Watched some of them die anyway—from the very heart failure the medication was supposed to prevent.

The Personal Toll

About three years ago, it happened to me.

My own blood pressure started creeping up. All those years of long shifts, stress, not taking care of myself the way I tell my patients to—it finally caught up with me.

At first it was just occasional high readings. Then it was consistent. 142/91. 145/93. 147/94.

My doctor pulled up my chart. Looked at the trend. And said the words I'd been dreading my entire adult life. "I'm prescribing Lisinopril. 10 milligrams daily."

My throat tightened. Lisinopril.

The same medication I'd watched destroy my mother's quality of life for nine years. Her fingers first—swelling so bad her wedding ring had to be cut off. Then her ankles. She went from heels to flats to house slippers.

Then the cough started. That dry, hacking cough that interrupted every conversation, woke her up at night, made her sound sick even though it was "just the medication." The dizziness got so bad she fell twice. Once in the kitchen. Once getting out of bed.

Her energy disappeared. A woman who used to host Sunday dinners for the whole family couldn't stay awake past 7 PM. And her kidneys. Year after year, her kidney function declined. Her doctor said it was "expected." Said they'd "monitor it." Creatinine climbing. GFR dropping. Stage 3. Then Stage 4. Then dialysis.

Three times a week. Four hours per session. For two years before her heart gave out anyway. My father's words at the funeral still echo: "The pills didn't save her. They just made her suffer longer."

Choosing a Different Path So when my doctor handed me that prescription, I smiled politely and said I'd think about it. But I knew I wasn't going to take it. I'd watched too many patients go down that road. I'd watched my own mother go down that road. I wasn't about to follow her path.

So I just dealt with it. Cut sodium. Lost fifteen pounds. Walked every morning. Tried to manage my stress. My numbers? Still 144/92 at my next checkup.

The doctor's office called. Then called again. "Ms. Warren, your blood pressure is Stage 2. Dr. Reeves needs confirmation you're taking the medication." I told them I needed more time. But the truth was, I didn't know what else to do.

Then about four months ago, one of my long-term patients came in for her regular appointment. Eleanor. Sweet woman, early sixties. High blood pressure for over a decade. I'd watched her struggle with it for years—tried everything, nothing gave her more than temporary improvement. She was on two medications and still barely controlled.

But this time she seemed different. Lighter somehow. I asked how she was managing, and she said something I didn't expect. "My numbers are actually good now. Like, consistently good. My doctor took me off one of my medications."

I almost dropped her chart. "What happened?"

She told me she'd found this beetroot supplement. But not just any beetroot—she was very specific about that. She'd tried the ones from the grocery store before. Those didn't do anything. This one was different. "I felt it working within the first half hour," she said. "And by the second month, my readings were better than they'd been in years."

The Science Behind the Solution

I'll be honest. I was skeptical. After 32 years as a nurse, I've heard every miracle cure story you can imagine. Supplements that promise the world and deliver nothing. Patients who swear something worked when really their numbers just fluctuated.

But Eleanor wasn't like that. She was sharp. Skeptical herself. And she seemed so genuinely relieved. Like she'd finally found something that actually worked.

That night, I checked my own blood pressure. 146/93. And I kept thinking about what she'd said. So I looked it up.

Turns out, the science on beetroot and blood pressure is real. Dietary nitrates convert to nitric oxide in your body—and nitric oxide is what tells your blood vessels to relax and dilate. Studies from Duke, Stanford, Johns Hopkins showing meaningful blood pressure reductions. Real universities. Real research. Not fringe pseudoscience.

But then I noticed something. People weren't just saying "beetroot supplements." They were getting specific about which ones.

One person said they tried four different brands before finding one that actually worked. Another mentioned that most beetroot supplements are basically worthless—something about the manufacturing process destroying the active compounds.

I started digging deeper. Turns out, most beetroot supplements use high-heat spray drying. It's cheap and fast. But heat destroys up to 90% of the dietary nitrates—the very compounds that lower blood pressure.

And the dosing? The clinical studies used 400 to 500 milligrams of nitrates daily. Most commercial supplements contain less than 50 milligrams. Some as low as 10. But they don't have to tell you that. The label just says "beetroot powder." No nitrate content disclosed.

So you're taking what you think is beetroot for your blood pressure, but you're really getting a tiny fraction of what actually works—and most of that has been destroyed by heat processing anyway. No wonder the supplements I'd seen patients try never seemed to do anything.

My Results with Rosabella I ordered one of the popular brands. Took it religiously for six weeks. My blood pressure? 143/91. Barely moved.

I tried another brand. The one with the TV commercials. Eight weeks. Didn't miss a single day. Still 142/90.

I was getting frustrated. The research said beetroot worked. But it wasn't working for me.

So I went back to what Eleanor had told me. She'd been specific about the brand. Said most of them didn't work—but this one did. Rosabella.

I looked them up, expecting to be disappointed again. But they were different.

Cold-extracted, not heat-processed. That preserves the nitrates instead of destroying them.

Standardized to 400 milligrams of nitrates per serving—the actual clinical dose.

Third-party tested, with published results. Certificate of Analysis available. You could see exactly what was in it.

No fillers. No additives. Just one ingredient—organic beetroot.

Made in the USA. My next checkup was in six weeks. If my numbers weren't down, I knew what was coming. The prescription. The path my mother walked.

I ordered it. When it arrived, I took the first dose that morning. Didn't expect much. I'd already been burned twice.

But about twenty minutes later, I noticed something. A lift. Like someone had turned up the oxygen in my blood. The mental fog I usually felt by mid-morning wasn't there. I felt... clear.

That evening, the exhaustion that usually hit me around 6 PM didn't come. I walked to the bathroom. Grabbed my blood pressure monitor. Wrapped the cuff. 141/89.

I stared at it. Checked again. 141/89. It was the first reading under 142 I'd seen in over a year.

One week later: 138/86. I started checking morning, afternoon, and evening. The numbers stayed consistent.

Two weeks: 134/84. My husband noticed I wasn't falling asleep on the couch after dinner anymore. Didn't say anything. Just watched me move around the house with energy I hadn't had in months.

Three weeks: 131/82.

Four weeks: 128/79. I checked it three times that morning because I didn't believe it.

The Final Verdict When I went back to Dr. Reeves for my checkup, the nurse took my blood pressure. Frowned. Took it again. Then a third time. "126 over 78."

Dr. Reeves pulled up my chart. Looked at the history. Looked at the new reading. Looked at me. "What happened?"

I told her. Cold-extracted beetroot. Standardized to the clinical dose. Third-party tested. The difference between what actually works and what just has "beetroot" on the label. She typed notes. Nodding slowly. "Well," she said, closing her laptop. "Whatever you're doing, keep doing it. These numbers are excellent. No Lisinopril necessary at this time."

No Lisinopril necessary.

I walked to my car. Sat there for a moment. Then called my husband. "126 over 78. No medication." I heard him exhale. Then a long silence. "Thank God, Linda. Thank God."

That was four months ago. My blood pressure this morning? 122/76. Normal. Healthy. No medication.

No persistent cough. No dizziness. No swollen ankles. No watching my kidney function decline year after year. No following my mother's path.

Last weekend my daughter asked me to go hiking with her and the grandkids. A real hike—not a flat walking path, but a trail with elevation. Three miles. Two years ago, I would have made an excuse. Would have been too worried about getting winded, about my blood pressure spiking, about not being able to keep up.

But I said yes. And I did it. The whole trail. My five-year-old grandson held my hand the last half mile, chattering about the birds and the bugs and asking if we could do this again next weekend. My eyes burned. Because the answer was yes.

Final Thoughts I'm sharing this because I hope it can help someone else the way it helped me.

If you're dealing with blood pressure that won't respond to diet and exercise. If your doctor is pushing medication you're terrified to take. If you've watched a parent or spouse suffer through the side effects and you're desperate not to follow that path.

If you've tried other beetroot supplements and they didn't work—now you know why. Most of them are heat-processed garbage with a fraction of the clinical dose.

Rosabella is different. Cold-extracted. 400 milligrams of nitrates. Third-party verified. The dose and the formulation that actually works.

title: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
My nephew been on me to post this for months now. I told him mind his business. He said somebody out here need to hear what happened. So here we go.

My name is Michael. Everybody call me Big Mike. 

I'm 61, and I just came home after four years away, and I want to tell y'all something real important about what happened at my own welcome home cookout.

Because what happened that day changed my life. And I believe it can change yours too.

My sister had been planning that cookout since the week I got my date. 

My nephew had the speakers set up before noon. My mama was in the kitchen making her potato salad the same way she's made it since '87, the one she never let nobody touch.

And I was at the grill. That's my spot. Always has been.

White tank, tongs in my hand, that smooth Al Green coming through the speakers. Everything exactly the way it was supposed to be on a day I'd been waiting for.

I’ll never forget the moment I picked up the grill brush to clean the rack before the chicken went on.

First brush. Second brush. Third brush.

Then my right arm stopped like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground and I grabbed the side of that grill to catch myself, and for one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

The whole yard went quiet. Everybody knew something was wrong.

Now look. 

I straightened up and told everybody I was fine and waved it off. 

Had my nephew take over the grill and went and sat in a lawn chair for the rest of the afternoon.

I barely ate. 

I held my arm against my chest the whole time, laughed when I was supposed to laugh, and acted like nothing happened.

You know what it is. 

And I'm telling you right now, that was the wrong thing to do.

Later that night, nephew sat down next to me and asked me straight what was going on.

I told him it had been happening. 

Not just the arm, but the headaches, the fatigue that didn't make sense, the mornings where my hands felt like they belonged to somebody else. 

He asked me if I'd seen a doctor.

I looked at him the way I've looked at that question my whole life.

"For what, some pills?" I said. "Same pills they gave pops. Same pills they gave Ray. You see how that worked out for them."

He didn't push it that night. But I could see it in his face when he left. 

The same look my daddy had the morning he drove my brother to the hospital and came home alone to tell us what happened.

That look stayed with me.

Now I want to be honest with y'all because that's what this is about.

I'd been on blood pressure medication before, years ago before I went in. 

And yes, the pills moved the number, but I still felt like I was running on half of what I used to have. 

I was heavy in the mornings, foggy all afternoon, and my damn hands was always cold. 

My doctor said my numbers looked fine. I told him I didn't feel fine. He said give it more time.

I know I ain't the only one who's gone through that.

Two weeks after the cookout my nephew showed up at my door on a Sunday with a bottle and a look on his face I recognized from when he was about nine years old and had done something he wasn't sure I was going to be okay with.

"Before you say anything," he said. "Just let me talk."

He sat at my kitchen table and walked me through everything he'd been reading for two weeks straight. 

About something called nitric oxide. 

Now y'all, I ain't never heard nothing like that in my life, but I let him keep going because I could see in his eyes he needed me to listen.

He explained how the body produces it naturally to tell blood vessels to relax and stay open on their own. 

How production drops every single year after 40 and by the time most of us men hit our late 50s we done lost the majority of what we had. 

How the blood pressure medication targets the pressure reading but don't do nothing about nitric oxide. Not a thing.

And how one of the most concentrated natural sources of the raw material your body needs to make nitric oxide is beetroot.

He slid the bottle across the table.

I picked it up. Read the label slow.

"Beetroot," I said.

"Beetroot," he said.

Now look. 

I sat there thinking about how a vegetable was supposed to do anything. 

I played defensive end. I ain't trying to hear nothing about no beetroot.

But I could tell this boy had done his homework.

He wasn't trying to sell me something. He was scared of losing me.

I said aight. Two capsules every morning with breakfast. Made him my word on it. He left the bottle and went home.

I didn't call him for two weeks. But I kept my word.

Called him on a Tuesday morning and the first thing out my mouth was, "Nephew. What is in this stuff?"

Because listen. 

For weeks my hands had been warm when I woke up. I ain't even notice how cold they'd been every single morning until the morning they weren't. 

That's how long I'd been living like that without knowing any different.

The heaviness in my chest started lifting, and I knew something was happening in my body that hadn't happened in years.

A few weeks later my sister called my nephew without telling me. 

Said she ain't know what was different but I seemed more like myself. Said there was a stillness she'd been watching in me ever since I came home that she hadn't said nothing about. 

And y'all, my sleep. 

I had forgot what it felt like to sleep all the way through the night without lying there with my thoughts running. I was sleeping for real.

About six weeks after our talk, nephew came by on a Sunday. 

I was in the driveway washing my truck, something I hadn't been able to do myself in over a year because standing that long wore me completely out.

He pulled up and sat in his car for a long second before he got out. I could see him through the windshield just watching me work.

He got out. Didn't say a word.

I looked up. "That stuff works, nephew."

Went right back to washing the truck.

Two months later we had another cookout.

I was at the grill the whole afternoon, didn't sit down one time, or come nowhere near putting my face in that potato salad.

And I want y'all to know something. 

I got people in my neighborhood on this now. 

My buddy Calvin, been on blood pressure medication eight years, his wife called me last month and said whatever you gave him she wants some too. 

My neighbor Ms. Trice, 68 years old, said she got more energy now than she had at 55. 

I got people asking me about this.

It's been about four months for me. 

I take it every morning. 

My blood pressure feels the most stable it's been in years, my hands are warm, and I get through a full day without counting down the hours till I need to sit down. 

I feel like myself again, and I'm old enough to know that ain't nothing small.

Now I'm not telling nobody to stop their medication or stop listening to their doctor. That ain't what this is about.

What I'm telling you is that there was something nephew found that nobody ever told me about, and it addressed the part of this that the pills never touched. 

And I believe if you been on blood pressure medication for years and you still don't feel right, this might be the thing nobody told you about either.

You are missing a blessing by not knowing about this. I really mean that.

The beetroot nephew found is called Rosabella. 

He found it after testing a whole bunch of products that didn't do nothing. 

Turns out most beetroot supplements on the market use high-heat processing that destroys the nitrates before they ever reach your body. 

Rosabella cold processes theirs so the nitrates are actually intact when you take it. 

Certified organic whole beetroot root, 1300mg per serving, one ingredient, third-party tested.

They got a 90-day money-back guarantee. If it doesn't do anything for you, you send it back. Simple as that.

But if it does what it did for me, you'll know. 

And then you gon' be doing what I'm doing, calling everybody you know.

I'm not somebody who posts things on the internet. Ask anybody who knows me. 

But nephew said somebody out here needs to hear this, and I believe he's right.

He pays for me to be on the subscription and sends it to my place. 

I keep telling him I'm going to pay him back, but he keeps telling me not to worry about it.

Check it out: 

get.tryrosabella.com/beetroot/subtxt/mm-hbp-ps

title: How BP meds nearly killed my wife
body:
She started taking blood pressure medication at 59. Within three months, I didn't recognize the woman I married.

She'd fall asleep on the couch at 7pm every night, sometimes with her book still open in her lap. 

She couldn't finish a conversation without losing her train of thought mid-sentence. I'd ask her about her day and she'd stare at me like she was trying to remember what happened.

She had zero interest in intimacy. Not just physically—she didn't want to hold hands anymore. Didn't call her friends. Didn't seem present when we were in the same room.

It was like living with a ghost of the woman I loved.

At first, I thought maybe she was depressed. Or going through menopause. Or that after 34 years of marriage, she'd just... given up on us. 

I'd try to talk to her about it, and she'd say, "I'm fine. Just tired."

But it wasn't normal tired. It was like someone had turned down the dimmer switch on her entire personality.

The woman who used to be on the phone with her sister every morning. Who'd stay up late organizing photo albums. 

Who had more energy than me most days. Gone. Replaced by someone who shuffled through the house like she was moving through fog.

The Doctor Visit That Made Me Furious

I finally convinced her to let me come to her next doctor's appointment. I wasn't going to let her brush this off as "just tired" anymore.

When the nurse called us back, I spoke up before she could. "Something's wrong," I said. "She's exhausted all the time. She can't think straight. 

She doesn't seem like herself. This isn't normal."

The doctor, the same one who'd been seeing my wife for 15 years, barely looked up from his computer screen. "How's your blood pressure been?" he asked her.

"Good. The medication is working."

The doctor nodded. "That's what matters. Your numbers are stable."

I cut in. "But what about everything else? The fatigue? The brain fog? The fact that she's barely functional?"

The doctor finally looked at me. "That's just part of getting older," he said. "The medication is keeping her cardiovascular system under control. 

We need to keep her on it to prevent a stroke or heart attack."

I felt my face get hot. "So she's supposed to feel like this for the rest of her life?"

"Would you rather she have a stroke?" I didn't have an answer for that.

We left with a prescription refill and a pamphlet on "healthy aging." I nearly teared-up in the car on the way home. My wife squeezed my hand and said, "It's okay. I'm fine."

But she wasn't fine. And I refused to accept that this was just "getting older."

What Blood Pressure Meds Actually Do to Your Body (And Why Doctors Don't Tell You)

That night, I couldn't sleep. 

I sat up in bed with my phone and started searching: "beta blocker side effects," "why do blood pressure meds make you tired," "is there an alternative to blood pressure medication."

What I found made me furious.

Beta blockers work by suppressing your cardiovascular system. They slow your heart rate. They reduce the force of each heartbeat. 

They literally tell your heart to work less hard. That's how they lower blood pressure.

But here's what the doctor never explained: When your heart pumps slower and weaker, less blood flows through your entire body. 

Less blood to your brain. Less oxygen to your muscles. Less circulation to your organs, your extremities, everywhere.

That's why women on beta blockers feel:

Constantly exhausted: Your body is running on reduced fuel. Your heart isn't pumping enough oxygenated blood to give you energy.

Mentally foggy: Your brain needs 20% of your body's blood supply to function. Cut that supply, and your thinking slows down. Memory gets worse. Processing speed drops.

Zero libido: Reduced blood flow affects everything, including that. Sexual function requires strong circulation. 

Beta blockers are notorious for causing loss of desire and inability to reach climax.

Cold hands and feet: Poor circulation means your extremities don't get enough warm blood. My wife's hands were like ice all the time.

Depressed and emotionally flat: When your body is running at half-speed, your mood follows. Everything feels harder. Nothing feels exciting.

The doctor said this was "just getting older." But my wife was 59. Not 89. And I couldn't accept that the rest of her life, OUR life together, had to look like this.

The Night I Found the Real Problem (And Why Doctors Ignore It)

I kept digging. 

I found a study published in the Journal of Clinical Hypertension that explained something his doctor never mentioned: High blood pressure isn't caused by your heart beating too hard. 

It's caused by your blood vessels being too tight.

Think about it like a garden hose. If the hose is wide open and relaxed, water flows through easily. Low pressure. 

If the hose is pinched and constricted, the pump has to work harder to push water through. High pressure.

Your blood vessels work the same way. And what controls whether your vessels stay open and relaxed? A molecule called nitric oxide.

When nitric oxide levels are healthy, your blood vessels stay wide, soft, and flexible. Blood flows freely. Your heart doesn't have to strain. Your pressure stays normal.

But after age 40, nitric oxide production drops about 10% every single year. By 55, you've lost nearly half of what you had at 30. Without nitric oxide, your blood vessels constrict. 

They get stiff and narrow. Your heart has to pump harder to push blood through those tight, resistant vessels. That's high blood pressure.

And here's the part that made me want to throw my phone across the room: Beta blockers don't fix the tight vessels. They just force your heart to slow down and pump weaker. 

It's like having a clogged pipe and responding by turning down the water pressure instead of clearing the blockage.

The blood pressure reading improves. But the underlying problem, the constricted vessels, gets worse. 

And your entire body suffers because it's not getting the blood flow it needs to function.

The study went on to explain that restoring nitric oxide is the key to actually fixing high blood pressure. Not managing it. Not suppressing it. Fixing it.

But there's no prescription drug that restores nitric oxide. Pharmaceutical companies can't patent it. Doctors can't bill for follow-up appointments to monitor it. 

There's no recurring revenue stream.

So they prescribe beta blockers instead. They turn your wife into a zombie. And they tell you it's "just part of getting older."

The Research That Gave Me Hope

I kept reading. The same study referenced clinical trials from the UK and research from Stanford that tested a natural solution: Dietary nitrates.

Certain vegetables, especially beetroot, contain high concentrations of nitrates that your body converts directly into nitric oxide. 

Researchers tested this on women over 50 with high blood pressure. No medication. No prescriptions. Just concentrated beetroot extract.

After 8 weeks: Average blood pressure dropped from 162/98 to 142/86. Participants reported significantly better energy levels. Mental clarity improved. 

Circulation improved—participants said their hands and feet weren't cold anymore. Sexual function improved. (The study used clinical terms, but the implication was clear.)

No side effects. No sedation. No brain fog. Because they weren't suppressing their cardiovascular system. 

They were restoring the molecule that tells blood vessels to relax and open naturally.

I found another study published in the Journal of Applied Physiology. Same results. 

Women who supplemented with beetroot nitrates saw an average drop of 8-10 points in systolic blood pressure within 4 weeks.

This wasn't fringe science or some sketchy internet claim. This was peer-reviewed research published in major medical journals.

So why didn't my wife's doctor tell her about it? Because there's no prescription to write. No follow-up appointments. No insurance billing codes. 

Just a vegetable that actually fixes the problem instead of masking it.

I woke my wife up at 2am. "I found something," I said.

What Happened When She Tried It

I showed her the studies. She was skeptical. "If this actually worked, my doctor would have told me about it."

"Would she?" I said. "There's no money in it for her."

My wife stared at the screen for a long time. Then she said, "What do we have to lose?"

The next morning, I started researching beetroot supplements. Here's what I learned fast: most of them are garbage. 

They're heat-processed, which destroys the nitrates before they even get into the bottle. They're loaded with fillers, binders, and added sugars. 

(Which is insane, because high blood sugar actively destroys nitric oxide. These products are literally making the problem worse.) They're dosed way too low to have any clinical effect.

The research used 400-500mg of nitrates per dose. Most supplements contain maybe 50mg.

I found one company that did it right: Cold-pressed to preserve the active nitrates. Single ingredient. Just organic beetroot. Nothing else. 

Dosed at 1,300mg per serving, which delivers the nitrate levels used in clinical research. Third-party lab verified. What's on the label is actually in the bottle. 

No sugars. No fillers. Because if your supplement spikes your blood glucose, it's destroying the very molecule you're trying to restore.

I ordered a bottle. My wife started taking it twice a day.

Week One: "I Slept Better Last Night"

The first few days, nothing. Then on day 5, my wife said something at breakfast. "I slept really well last night."

I didn't think much of it. But that night, she stayed awake past 7pm. We watched a movie together. She didn't fall asleep halfway through.

The next morning, she was up at 6am. Not shuffling around like usual. Actually awake.

"How do you feel?" I asked.

"I don't know," she said. "Maybe a little better?"

Week Two: "I Feel Like I Can Think Again"

By the end of the second week, the change was undeniable. She wasn't losing her train of thought mid-conversation anymore. 

She remembered things. Little details I'd mentioned in passing days earlier.

She made a joke at dinner—an actual joke, with timing and a punchline. I almost cried.

"Your hands aren't cold," I said, holding them.

She looked down. "Huh. You're right."

Week Four: "I Feel Like Myself Again"

A month in, my wife woke me up early one Saturday morning. "Let's go somewhere," she said. "I feel like driving up the coast."

We hadn't done that in over a year. We spent the whole day together. Talking, laughing, holding hands while we walked on the beach.

That night, lying in bed, she turned to me and said something I'll never forget: "I feel like myself again." She paused. 

"I didn't realize how far gone I was until I started coming back."

I didn't say anything. I just held her.

Week Six: The Doctor's Appointment

My wife had a routine follow-up with her doctor. I made her promise to let me come with her.

When the nurse checked her blood pressure, she looked at the reading twice. "144 over 88," she said. Down from 162/98.

The doctor came in and reviewed the chart. "Your pressure's looking good. The medication is working."

"Actually," my wife said, "I think it's the beetroot."

The doctor looked confused. "Beetroot?"

My wife explained what we'd been doing.

The doctor frowned. "Well, whatever you're doing, your numbers are stable. Keep it up."

My wife asked the question I'd been hoping she'd ask: "Can I reduce the beta blocker?"

The doctor hesitated. You could see her weighing liability versus common sense. Finally, she said, "Let's cut it in half and see how you do."

Two Months Later: Off the Beta Blocker Completely

My wife went back for another check. Blood pressure: 138/84. Normal range.

The doctor agreed to take her off the beta blocker entirely. "Monitor it at home," she said. "If it spikes, we'll need to put you back on."

But it didn't spike. It stayed stable. And I had my wife back. The woman who wakes up early. Who tells jokes. Who's present when we talk. 

Who reaches for my hand without me having to ask. The woman I married.

Why Most Beetroot Supplements Don't Work (And How to Avoid Wasting Your Money)

After we got results, I told everyone I knew. My brother's wife was on blood pressure meds. My neighbor. Three men from my poker group whose wives were in the same boat.

They all tried beetroot supplements. Two of them came back to me a month later and said, "It didn't work." I asked what brand they used. Both had bought cheap supplements from the drugstore or Amazon.

Here's the problem: Most beetroot products are complete garbage. They use high-heat processing that destroys the nitrates before they even go into the capsule. 

You're basically taking powdered beet dust. They're loaded with fillers like rice flour, magnesium stearate, and maltodextrin. Some even have added sugars. 

(Which actively destroys nitric oxide and makes your blood pressure worse.) They're dosed too low. Clinical research uses 400-500mg of active nitrates per dose. 

Most cheap supplements contain maybe 50mg. You'd need to take 10 capsules to match the research dose. They're not third-party tested. What's on the label isn't what's in the bottle.

title: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
My nephew been on me to post this for months now. I told him mind his business. He said somebody out here need to hear what happened. So here we go.

My name is Michael. Everybody call me Big Mike. 

I'm 61, and I just came home after four years away, and I want to tell y'all something real important about what happened at my own welcome home cookout.

Because what happened that day changed my life. And I believe it can change yours too.

My sister had been planning that cookout since the week I got my date. 

My nephew had the speakers set up before noon. My mama was in the kitchen making her potato salad the same way she's made it since '87, the one she never let nobody touch.

And I was at the grill. That's my spot. Always has been.

White tank, tongs in my hand, that smooth Al Green coming through the speakers. Everything exactly the way it was supposed to be on a day I'd been waiting for.

I’ll never forget the moment I picked up the grill brush to clean the rack before the chicken went on.

First brush. Second brush. Third brush.

Then my right arm stopped like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground and I grabbed the side of that grill to catch myself, and for one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

The whole yard went quiet. Everybody knew something was wrong.

Now look. 

I straightened up and told everybody I was fine and waved it off. 

Had my nephew take over the grill and went and sat in a lawn chair for the rest of the afternoon.

I barely ate. 

I held my arm against my chest the whole time, laughed when I was supposed to laugh, and acted like nothing happened.

You know what it is. 

And I'm telling you right now, that was the wrong thing to do.

Later that night, nephew sat down next to me and asked me straight what was going on.

I told him it had been happening. 

Not just the arm, but the headaches, the fatigue that didn't make sense, the mornings where my hands felt like they belonged to somebody else. 

He asked me if I'd seen a doctor.

I looked at him the way I've looked at that question my whole life.

"For what, some pills?" I said. "Same pills they gave pops. Same pills they gave Ray. You see how that worked out for them."

He didn't push it that night. But I could see it in his face when he left. 

The same look my daddy had the morning he drove my brother to the hospital and came home alone to tell us what happened.

That look stayed with me.

Now I want to be honest with y'all because that's what this is about.

I'd been on blood pressure medication before, years ago before I went in. 

And yes, the pills moved the number, but I still felt like I was running on half of what I used to have. 

I was heavy in the mornings, foggy all afternoon, and my damn hands was always cold. 

My doctor said my numbers looked fine. I told him I didn't feel fine. He said give it more time.

I know I ain't the only one who's gone through that.

Two weeks after the cookout my nephew showed up at my door on a Sunday with a bottle and a look on his face I recognized from when he was about nine years old and had done something he wasn't sure I was going to be okay with.

"Before you say anything," he said. "Just let me talk."

He sat at my kitchen table and walked me through everything he'd been reading for two weeks straight. 

About something called nitric oxide. 

Now y'all, I ain't never heard nothing like that in my life, but I let him keep going because I could see in his eyes he needed me to listen.

He explained how the body produces it naturally to tell blood vessels to relax and stay open on their own. 

How production drops every single year after 40 and by the time most of us men hit our late 50s we done lost the majority of what we had. 

How the blood pressure medication targets the pressure reading but don't do nothing about nitric oxide. Not a thing.

And how one of the most concentrated natural sources of the raw material your body needs to make nitric oxide is beetroot.

He slid the bottle across the table.

I picked it up. Read the label slow.

"Beetroot," I said.

"Beetroot," he said.

Now look. 

I sat there thinking about how a vegetable was supposed to do anything. 

I played defensive end. I ain't trying to hear nothing about no beetroot.

But I could tell this boy had done his homework.

He wasn't trying to sell me something. He was scared of losing me.

I said aight. Two capsules every morning with breakfast. Made him my word on it. He left the bottle and went home.

I didn't call him for two weeks. But I kept my word.

Called him on a Tuesday morning and the first thing out my mouth was, "Nephew. What is in this stuff?"

Because listen. 

For weeks my hands had been warm when I woke up. I ain't even notice how cold they'd been every single morning until the morning they weren't. 

That's how long I'd been living like that without knowing any different.

The heaviness in my chest started lifting, and I knew something was happening in my body that hadn't happened in years.

A few weeks later my sister called my nephew without telling me. 

Said she ain't know what was different but I seemed more like myself. Said there was a stillness she'd been watching in me ever since I came home that she hadn't said nothing about. 

And y'all, my sleep. 

I had forgot what it felt like to sleep all the way through the night without lying there with my thoughts running. I was sleeping for real.

About six weeks after our talk, nephew came by on a Sunday. 

I was in the driveway washing my truck, something I hadn't been able to do myself in over a year because standing that long wore me completely out.

He pulled up and sat in his car for a long second before he got out. I could see him through the windshield just watching me work.

He got out. Didn't say a word.

I looked up. "That stuff works, nephew."

Went right back to washing the truck.

Two months later we had another cookout.

I was at the grill the whole afternoon, didn't sit down one time, or come nowhere near putting my face in that potato salad.

And I want y'all to know something. 

I got people in my neighborhood on this now. 

My buddy Calvin, been on blood pressure medication eight years, his wife called me last month and said whatever you gave him she wants some too. 

My neighbor Ms. Trice, 68 years old, said she got more energy now than she had at 55. 

I got people asking me about this.

It's been about four months for me. 

I take it every morning. 

My blood pressure feels the most stable it's been in years, my hands are warm, and I get through a full day without counting down the hours till I need to sit down. 

I feel like myself again, and I'm old enough to know that ain't nothing small.

Now I'm not telling nobody to stop their medication or stop listening to their doctor. That ain't what this is about.

What I'm telling you is that there was something nephew found that nobody ever told me about, and it addressed the part of this that the pills never touched. 

And I believe if you been on blood pressure medication for years and you still don't feel right, this might be the thing nobody told you about either.

You are missing a blessing by not knowing about this. I really mean that.

The beetroot nephew found is called Rosabella. 

He found it after testing a whole bunch of products that didn't do nothing. 

Turns out most beetroot supplements on the market use high-heat processing that destroys the nitrates before they ever reach your body. 

Rosabella cold processes theirs so the nitrates are actually intact when you take it. 

Certified organic whole beetroot root, 1300mg per serving, one ingredient, third-party tested.

They got a 90-day money-back guarantee. If it doesn't do anything for you, you send it back. Simple as that.

But if it does what it did for me, you'll know. 

And then you gon' be doing what I'm doing, calling everybody you know.

I'm not somebody who posts things on the internet. Ask anybody who knows me. 

But nephew said somebody out here needs to hear this, and I believe he's right.

He pays for me to be on the subscription and sends it to my place. 

I keep telling him I'm going to pay him back, but he keeps telling me not to worry about it.

Check it out: 

get.tryrosabella.com/beetroot/subtxt/mm-hbp-ps

title: Support stamina and heart health every day
body:
My nephew been on me to post this for two months. I told him it wasn't nobody's business. He said it could save somebody's life. So here I am.

I think I had a stroke last June. At my own welcome home cookout. 

Four years away, first day back with the whole family, and I grabbed the grill brush, got two strokes in, and my right arm just stopped working like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground. 

I grabbed the side of the grill to catch myself. 

For one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

My nephew cut the music. The whole yard went quiet.

And that's when I knew how bad it was, because Al Green don't stop at a cookout unless something is really wrong.

But that day didn't come out of nowhere, so let me back up.

About two years before that cookout, while I was still away, I started noticing things weren't right. 

I was heavy in the mornings like I hadn't slept at all, my hands and feet were always cold, and there was this fog sitting in my head every day that I couldn't shake. 

There was also this weight in my chest that wasn't really pain, just weight, and I couldn't explain it to nobody because I didn't know how to name something I'd never felt before.

I asked around. Talked to whoever I could. 

Everybody said the same thing: that's just getting older. 

That wasn’t good enough for me, so I tried what I could. 

CoQ10, garlic, magnesium, cut my salt way down. 

If anything, I felt worse as the months went by.

Then about six months before the cookout I started waking up in the middle of the night, wide awake with this low-grade dread sitting on me for no reason I could name, feeling like my body was trying to tell me something I couldn't figure out. 

And still, nobody around me could tell me why.

Then came June.

My sister had been planning that cookout since the week I got my date. 

My nephew had the speakers up before noon. My mama was in the kitchen making her potato salad the same way she's made it since '87, the one nobody’s allowed to touch.

And I was on the grill. 

That's my spot, always has been.

White tank, tongs in my hand, that smooth Al Green coming through the speakers. Everything exactly the way it was supposed to be on a day I'd been waiting four years for.

I picked up the grill brush to clean the rack before the chicken went on.

First stroke. Second stroke. Third stroke.

My right arm stopped. Like somebody cut the power to it.

The brush hit the ground and I grabbed the side of that grill to catch myself, and for one second I was looking straight at my mama's potato salad coming up to meet my face.

My nephew came running, took one look at me, and cut the music. 

The whole yard froze. 

And that's when I knew how bad it was.

I waved everybody off, told them I was fine, had my nephew take over the grill and went and sat in a lawn chair for the rest of the afternoon. 

I barely ate, held my arm against my chest the whole time, laughed when I was supposed to laugh, and acted like nothing happened.

You know how it goes.

I went home that night and asked around again. 

Called people I trusted, people who'd been dealing with blood pressure stuff for years. 

Nobody had a real answer, but everybody had a theory.

Too much heat, dehydration, and pushing yourself too hard. 

But nobody could tell me why my arm just stopped like that, and I could tell by the way people were talking that they were scared too, they just didn't want to say it out loud.

So I went home terrified. 

Terrified to go to sleep, terrified my arm was going to do it again but next time there'd be nothing to grab. 

What if it happened when I was driving? What if nobody was around? 

I'd watched my pops go down from something like this. My brother Ray. I knew what road this looked like and I knew where it ended.

I started waking up, wiggling my fingers just to make sure they still worked. 

My nephew kept calling and I kept telling him I was fine, but I wasn't fine and we both knew it.

He drove over two weeks after the cookout. 

When he walked in I could see it in his face, he thought he was going to lose me. 

First cookout back and this is what happened.

We sat at my kitchen table and I tried to act normal, but my hands were cold when I poured the drinks and he noticed.

"Unc," he said quietly. "You're not okay."

I didn't say nothing for a long time. Then I told him everything.

The 3am, fingers in the dark, and terror of knowing something was going wrong in my body and not being able to stop it or name it or find anybody who could tell me what it was.

He didn't say anything for a long time. Then he said: "I'm going to figure this out."

Four days later he called, voice urgent. "Unc. I think I found something."

When he showed up that weekend he was carrying a bottle.

"Before you say anything," he said. "Just let me talk."

He pulled out Rosabella Organic Beetroot and sat back down at that kitchen table and walked me through everything he'd been reading for two weeks straight. 

About something called nitric oxide.

I ain't never heard nothing like that in my life, but I let him keep going because I could see in his eyes he needed me to listen. 

He explained how the body makes it naturally to keep blood vessels relaxed and open on their own, how it declines every year after 40, how by the time most men get to where I am we done lost most of what we had, and how that decline was connected to everything I'd been feeling.

The heaviness, the cold hands, the fog, the way my circulation had been quietly slowing down for years without me having any name for it.

He said the blood pressure pills they always want to put everybody on deal with the reading but they don't do a thing about that. 

And that one of the most concentrated natural sources of what the body needs to actually make nitric oxide is beetroot.

He slid the bottle across the table. I picked it up and read the label slow.

"Beetroot," I said.

"Beetroot," he said.

Now look. I played defensive end. I ain't trying to hear nothing about no beetroot. 

But I could tell this boy had done his homework, and more than that I could see something in his face I hadn't seen since he was a little boy: fear. 

Not fear of the supplement. Fear of losing me.

This boy who used to sit in the front row at every one of my high school football games. 

Who wrote me letters the whole four years without missing one. 

Who planned that cookout with his mama because he wanted me to come home to something that felt right.

He wasn't trying to sell me something. He was scared.

"Unc I can't watch you go out like pops and Ray. Please."

I said aight.

He told me two capsules every morning with breakfast, made me promise, left the bottle and went home. I didn't call him for two weeks but I kept my word.

Called him on a Tuesday morning and the first thing out my mouth was: "Nephew… What is in this stuff."

Because listen, for weeks my hands had been warm when I woke up, and I ain't even notice how cold they'd been every single morning until the morning they weren't. 

That's how long I'd been living like that without knowing any different.

The heaviness in my chest started lifting too, and something was going on in my body that I couldn't explain, I just knew it was different than it had been in years.

Couple weeks later my sister called my nephew without telling me. 

Said she ain't know what was different but I seemed more like myself, and that there was a stillness she'd been watching in me since I came home that she hadn't said nothing about.

And my sleep got right too. 

I had forgot what it felt like to sleep all the way through without lying there with my thoughts running.

About six weeks in, nephew came by on a Sunday and I was in the driveway washing my truck, something I hadn't done myself in over a year because standing that long wore me out. 

He pulled up and sat in his car for a second before he got out, and I could see him through the windshield just watching me work. 

He got out and didn't say a word. I looked up.

"That stuff works, nephew."

Went right back to washing the truck.

Two months later we had another cookout. 

I was at the grill the whole afternoon, white tank, tongs in my hand, didn't sit down once, didn't come nowhere near that potato salad. 

And for me that's when I knew for real.

It's been about four months now. I take it every morning. 

My blood pressure feels the most stable it's been in years, at least I think. 

My hands are warm. 

I get through a full day without counting down the hours. 

I feel like myself again, and I'm old enough to know that ain't nothing small.

I'm not telling nobody what to do with their health. 

What I'm telling you is that my nephew found something that nobody in my community had ever told me about, and it addressed something that nothing else had touched, and I believe if you been feeling the way I was feeling you deserve to know about it too. 

Because that's how we do. 

When something is real, you tell the people you care about.

I got my neighbor Calvin on it, been dealing with blood pressure stuff for eight years and called me two weeks in saying something was different. 

Got my neighbor Ms. Trice on it, she's 68 and said she got more energy than she can remember having in years. 

I been telling everybody I know.

Before you go looking for a beetroot supplement though, I need to tell you something. 

Most of the ones on Amazon and at the regular stores don't work, and my nephew found that out the hard way testing all of them. 

Most brands use high-heat processing to make it fast and cheap, and that destroys the nitrates before they ever reach your body, so you're basically paying for red powder in a capsule with nothing left in it that matters.

Rosabella cold processes theirs so the nitrates are actually intact when you take it. 

Certified organic whole beetroot root, 1300mg per serving, one ingredient, third-party tested.

They got a 90-day money-back guarantee, so if it doesn't do anything for you, you send it back. 

But if it does what it did for me, you'll know.

I'm not somebody who posts things on the internet. Ask anybody who knows me. 

But nephew said somebody out here needs to hear this, and I believe he's right.

He put me on a subscription after the second month. 

Brings it to the house himself. I keep telling him I'm going to pay him back, but he keeps telling me not to worry about it.
